In the decades since my initiation into the world of high stakes negotiations, I've been continually surprised by how viable these seemingly simple approaches can be. The ability to get inside the head of your counterpart depends on these techniques and your willingness to adapt your strategy based on new evidence. I always try to reinforce the message that being right isn't the key to successful negotiation. Having the right approach is. The language of negotiation is primarily the language of conversation and rapport, a way of quickly establishing relationships and getting people to talk and think together. What separates a good negotiator from a great one is that a great negotiator aims to use his or her skills to uncover surprises he or she is certain to find. Good negotiators tend to be afraid of surprises. It's very important never to commit to assumptions, instead of view them as hypotheses that you are out to test and prove or disprove. The art of negotiation is nothing more than a process of discovery and the building of trust based influence. Your goal should be to uncover as much information as possible. To maximize this discovery process, make your soul in all encompassing focus on the other person and what they have to say. When you do have to speak, slow it down. Going too fast is a mistake most negotiators are prone to making. This just adds to the anxiety everyone is feeling and makes your counterpart feel as though they aren't being heard which undermines the trust based influence you've worked so hard to build. Remember to smile. Your brain is 31% more powerful in a positive frame of mind. It helps you think more quickly. It also makes your counterpart both smarter and simultaneously more collaborative. They are more likely to problem solve with you instead of against you. Don't forget the importance of the tone you are using, how you say something is roughly five times more important than what you are saying. They will hear your intention and attitude in your tone. The three most common voice tones available to negotiators are the late night FM DJ voice, the positive playful voice and the assertive voice which should almost never be used. Make your inflection go upward or downward to put the finishing touch on your unspoken message. Lastly mirrors work magic just repeating the last three words or the critical one to three words of what someone just said. Mary encourages the other side to keep talking and buys you time to regroup and encourages your counterpart to reveal their strategy. You feel awkward at first but if you practice enough eventually you'll master this technique and be able to employ it in a variety of settings.
